title: Macquarie chief’s pay slashed as board clocks regulatory mood
full_text:
Macquarie Group chief executive Shemara Wikramanayake had her pay docked by $7m, despite the global financial giant posting its second largest profit ever.
The commodities, banking and advisory powerhouse recorded a 30 per cent lift in earnings after posting a $4.85bn annual profit on Friday, outstripping analysts expectations of around $4.4bn.
It marks the group’s second-highest profit ever recorded, behind $5.18bn in 2023.
Despite this, CEO Ms Wikramanayake had her pay packet reduced by $7m, to account for shareholders’ regulatory and risk concerns after Macquarie suffered its first strike at last year’s AGM. She was still paid $26.5m, up from $24m last year.
Board chair Glenn Stevens said the hit to Ms Wikramanayake’s pay was because they had listened to investors who “felt that the remuneration adjustments we made for risk measures were inadequate”.
In the past year, Macquarie was fined $35m for misreporting millions of short sales and the corporate regulator imposed additional conditions on Macquarie Bank’s AFS licence over compliance issues.
The commodities and global markets unit, Macquarie’s largest division, performed the best, delivering a net profit contribution of $4.22bn, up almost 50 per cent on the previous year.
This was largely driven by the sale of European meter asset provider OnStream and the increased risk management income from client hedging activity across its global gas, power and oil businesses. As a consequence, the head of the commodities business, Simon Wright, was paid $35.4m, an almost $13m increase on the previous year and more than the CEO.
Its corporate advisory arm, Macquarie Capital, had a 43 per cent jump in profit to $1.04bn, reflected by higher income from equity investments, mergers and acquisitions fees, brokerage and its private credit portfolio.
Its asset management division recorded a 27 per cent lift in profits to $2.6bn compared to the previous year, while its banking decision was up 17 per cent at $1.61bn.
“Each of our businesses used its specialist expertise in navigating the current environment, identifying opportunities that support long-term growth and delivering positive outcomes for our clients and communities,” Ms Wikramanayake said.
Underlining its stature as a global business, Macquarie said that 68 per cent of its total income was from international earnings.
The bank will pay a final dividend of $4.20 a share, 35 per cent franked, on July 2.
Jarden analyst Matt Wilson welcomed the result, saying he “expect upgrades by the Street”.
“[Macquarie] has transitioned the portfolio to be front and centre of a new paradigm of growth – where materials and matter outweigh financialisation. All divisions are now headed in the right direction,” he told clients.
Citi analyst Thomas Strong said the beat would probably trigger a 2 per cent upside to revenue in the next financial year, which currently sits at about $19.5bn.
Macquarie said it continued to maintain a cautious stance, in the face of rising interest rates and the ongoing conflict in the Middle East.
“Macquarie remains well-positioned to deliver superior performance in the medium term with established, diverse income streams; deep expertise across diverse sectors in major markets with structural growth tailwinds; patient adjacent growth across new products and new markets; ongoing investment in our operating platform; a strong and conservative balance sheet; and a proven risk management framework and culture,” Ms Wikramanayake said.
However, climate lobby group Market Forces branded Macquarie Australia’s “drill baby drill” bank after it had tripled finance for oil and gas projects over the past four years.
“Despite a record-breaking 35 per cent of shareholder votes cast in favour of clearer climate action from Macquarie last year, the bank has failed investors by radically increasing its support for fossil fuel expansion,” Market Forces banks analyst Morgan Pickett said. The group also announced that former RBA board member Jillian Broadbent would be stepping down from the board in December after eight years. Macquarie shares closed at $239.23, down 1 per cent.
summary: Macquarie Group CEO Shemara Wikramanayake had her pay reduced by $7 million to $26.5 million despite the company reporting a $4.85 billion profit, reflecting shareholder concerns over regulatory issues.

heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: Macquarie posted a 30% increase in earnings, reaching $4.85 billion in profit.
Wikramanayake's pay cut was influenced by shareholder feedback following regulatory fines and compliance issues.
Macquarie's commodities division saw significant profit growth, contributing $4.22 billion.
relevance: This news highlights the impact of regulatory concerns on executive compensation and company performance, which is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals to consider in risk assessment and corporate governance.
